frontmatter.title: Toposes are adhesive
frontmatter.meta: @inproceedings{lack-sobocinski-2006, address = {Berlin, Heidelberg}, author = {Lack, Stephen and Soboci{\'{n}}ski, Pawe{\l}}, booktitle = {Graph Transformations}, editor = {Corradini, Andrea and Ehrig, Hartmut and Montanari, Ugo and Ribeiro, Leila and Rozenberg, Grzegorz}, isbn = {978-3-540-38872-2}, pages = {184--198}, publisher = {Springer Berlin Heidelberg}, title = {Toposes Are Adhesive}, year = {2006} }

mainmatter.p: Adhesive categories have recently been proposed as a categorical foundation for facets of the theory of graph transformation, and have also been used to study techniques from process algebra for reasoning about concurrency. Here we continue our study of adhesive categories by showing that toposes are adhesive. The proof relies on exploiting the relationship between adhesive categories, Brown and Janelidze’s work on generalised van Kampen theorems as well as Grothendieck’s theory of descent.

Let \mathcal{C} be an adhesive category with a strict initial object, so that \mathcal{C} is also extensive. Then independent sums are created by the inclusion \mathbf{Inj} { \left ( \mathcal{C} \right )} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{C}.
mainmatter.block:
Proof.
Given a coproduct co-span { \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u+ v \hookleftarrow v \right \} } in \mathcal{C} along with an independent co-span { \left \{ u \hookrightarrow w \hookleftarrow v \right \} }, it suffices to check that the universal map u+ v \to w is a monomorphism. Equivalently, we wish to check that the following square is cartesian:
By the descent property for binary coproducts in an extensive category, it suffices to check that each of the following restrictions of that square along the coproduct injections is cartsian:
We will consider only the first square, which we rotate ninety degrees.
We see that the square above can be composed as follows so that the left-hand square is cocartesian and the outer square is cartesian.
Observing that all the vertical maps are monomoprhism, we use the “special pullback-pushout property” of Lack and Sobociński for adhesive categories to conclude that the right-hand component is cartesian. Thus it follows that u+v \to w is a monomorphism, and so we may define the independent coproduct { \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u \oplus v \hookleftarrow v \right \} } in \mathbf{Inj} { \left ( \mathcal{C} \right )} by the coproduct co-span { \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u+v \hookleftarrow v \right \} } in \mathcal{C}.

mainmatter.p: Suppose that the illustrated commutative diagram in an adhesive category has m, n, and l mono. Suppose that (1) is a pushout and (1)+(2) is a pullback; then (2) is a pullback.
